coredata.dc:title: Yellow-emitting carbon dots for selective detecting 4-NP in aqueous media and living biological imaging
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Tian, Min
Liu, Yaoming
Wang, Yingte
Zhang, Yong
coredata.dc:description: A facile and economical hydrothermal approach is reported for preparing fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) via using o-phenylenediamine and dicyandiamine. Herein, the bright-yellow-emissive N-CDs are uniformly dispersed spherical nanoparticles with favorable solubility, superior photoluminescence and photobleaching resistance. The fluorescence intensity of N-CDs is linearly quenched by 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) over a concentration range of 0.1–39 μM, corresponding to a detection limit (LOD) of 0.05 μM. Based on this phenomenon, a 4-NP-detection method is exploited and applied to real samples analysis. The synthesized N-CDs are highly biocompatible and capable of biological imaging. Therefore, they are excellent candidates for live biological imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Yellow-fluorescent
Nitrogen-doped carbon dots
4-nitrophenol detection
Biological imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Application of N-CDs to biological imaging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM and HRTEM (inset) image of N-CDs. (b) Size distribution of N-CDs. (c) Raman spectrum of the N-CDs. (d) FT-IR spectrum of the N-CDs.
(a) XPS survey scan spectrum. (b) C1S. (c) N1S. (d) O1S scan spectrum of the N-CDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption and fluorescence excitation/emission spectra of the N-CDs. Inset: images of the N-CDs under daylight (left) and UV irradiation (right). (b) Fluorescence spectra of the N-CDs at different excitation wavelengths.

Fluorescence intensity of N-CDs with 4-NP and different (a) Metal ions. (b) Organics in pH 7.0 PBS buffer. (Concentrations of different metal ions and organics are all 30 μM and 4-NP is 10 μM, CN-CDs = 0.13 mg/mL, λex = 440 nm).
(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of the N-CDs with various concentrations of 4-NP (0–200 μM). (b) The relationship between F0/F and the concentration of 4-NP within the linear range of 0.1–39 μM.
Viability (%) of O.laustris after incubating with N-CDs (3.5 h) evaluated from MTT assay.

(A) Control: (a–c) Fluorescence images of O.laustris cultured without N-CDs under yellow channel, bright field and the overlay of (a) and (b), respectively. (d–f) Fluorescence images of O.laustris cultured with N-CDs under yellow channel, bright field and the overlay of (d) and (e), respectively. (B) Dynamic fluorescence images of O.laustris obtained after adding 4-NP for 8 s (a), 16 s (b), 24 s (c), 32 s (d), 40 s (e), 48 s (f), respectively.
The synthesis of N-CDs and applications.
Comparison of various methods for 4-NP determination.

row.entry: DTD/Ag NP based carbon paste electrode

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Quantitative analysis of 4-NP in Tap water (n = 5).

head.ce:title: Yellow-emitting carbon dots for selective detecting 4-NP in aqueous media and living biological imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Scientific Instrument Center, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Scientific Instrument Center, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A facile and economical hydrothermal approach is reported for preparing fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) via using o-phenylenediamine and dicyandiamine. Herein, the bright-yellow-emissive N-CDs are uniformly dispersed spherical nanoparticles with favorable solubility, superior photoluminescence and photobleaching resistance. The fluorescence intensity of N-CDs is linearly quenched by 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) over a concentration range of 0.1–39 μM, corresponding to a detection limit (LOD) of 0.05 μM. Based on this phenomenon, a 4-NP-detection method is exploited and applied to real samples analysis. The synthesized N-CDs are highly biocompatible and capable of biological imaging. Therefore, they are excellent candidates for live biological imaging.

A novel yellow-emitting nitrogen-doped carbon dots have been successfully prepared using o-phenylenediamine and dicyandiamine as reaction precursors via one step hydrothermal-assisted method. The fluorescence intensity of N-CDs is selectively quenched by 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) due to the formation of a stable non-fluorescent ground-state complex. Based on this phenomenon, a highly sensitive 4-NP-detection method is established and applied to actual samples analysis. In addition, this probe can better employ for multimodal biological imaging.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: 4-Nitrophenol (4-NP) is mainly applied in organic synthesis and dyes, intermediates of medicines and pesticides, acid–base indicators and leather preservatives. However, when entering the sewage discharge from chemical enterprises, nitrophenols are pollutants that degrade the quality of surface water and groundwater [1]. Moreover, it is difficult to downgrade owing to its strong stability [2,3]. Thus, green approaches for special sensing 4-NP are urgently required.

4-NP is detected by a range of analytical methods such as chromatography [4,5], chemiluminescence [6–8], electrochemical [9] and electrophoresis methods. Nevertheless, the applicability of above-mentioned methods to pollution detection is hampered by the complicated instruments, tedious pretreatment procedures, expensive testing, and the production of secondary pollutants.

Carbon dots (CDs) are an emerging fluorescent nanomaterial that comprise microspherical, highly monodispersed particles with sizes below 10 nm. Compared with conventional semiconductor quantum dots and dyes, CDs hold a distinct combination of numerous unique features, such as favorable solubility, chemical stability, tunable photoluminescence and the excellent biosafety [10–18]. By virtue of their excellent properties, CDs have enabled unprecedented progress in drug carriers, sensing, biological imaging and catalysis [19,20]. The preparation methods of CDs depend on their carbon sources, but are broadly divisible into top-down and bottom-up strategies [21,22]. Generally speaking, the former is mainly electrochemical synthesis [23], laser ablation [24], discharge synthesis [25], and direct carbonation [26]. The latter primarily consist of pyrolysis [27], microwave-assisted procedures [28], wet oxidation [29], and hydrothermal synthesis [30]. Out of these methods, hydrothermal-assisted strategy is considered as a cost-effective preparation method.

Nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) with long wavelength emission have greatly expanded the applications of CDs in biological correlation analysis, on account of their weaker light scattering effect, stronger tissue penetration ability, and less background interference than short-wavelength emitters [31]. Liu et al. [32] demonstrated the capability of red-emission B-, N-, and S-co-doped CDs in live cell imaging. Jiang et al. [33] obtained red, green, and blue N-CDs for multicolor cellular imaging by a one-step hydrothermal approach. Liu et al. [34] fabricated yellow emission O-CDs and applied them to cell imaging. Currently, fluorescence CDs have been reported for the detection of 4-NP [35–38]. However, obtained CDs suffer from the expensive raw materials, complicated separation process and short wavelength emission. Thus, it is of urgent need to develop a facile method to prepare water-soluble and biocompatible CDs for sensing 4-NP.

In this work, an efficient aqueous synthesis of N-CDs was proposed using o-phenylenediamine and dicyandiamine as reaction precursors via one step hydrothermal-assisted method (Scheme.1 ). The synthesized N-CDs hold large Stokes shift, excitation-independent photoluminescent behavior, remarkable water-solubility and pH dependence. A 4-NP-detection method, based on the formation of a stable non-fluorescent ground-state N-CDs–4-NP complex, is then established and applied to actual samples analysis. Additionally, the prepared N-CDs are successfully employed for biological imaging. We expect that this potential bio-nano-material (N-CDs) will expand their practical applications of biomedical, disease diagnosis and bioimaging fields.

Mixing the o-phenylenediamine (0.5 g) and dicyandiamine (0.33 g) into ethanol (15 mL) and stirring. After that, mixtures were put into reaction kettle (100 ml) with maintaining for 9 h at 200 °C. The resulting raw products, cooled to room temperature, were centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 30 min and dialyzed with dialysis bag (molecular weight cutoff ≈ 1000 Da, for 48 h). Finally, brown fluorescent solid-state N-CDs were prepared by drying for further characterization.

Fluorescence studies were performed under the following procedures. In brief, 10 μL of N-CDs solution (CN-CDs = 0.13 mg/mL) was mixed with 1 μL of various concentrations of 4-NP solution (0, 2.0, 4.5, 7.0, 9.5, 14.0, 17.0, 20.0, 26.0, 30.0, 38.0, 56.0, 58.0, 60.0, 75.0, 85.0, 110.0, 135.0, 142.0, 160.0, 185.0, 200.0 μM), and then the mixed solution was diluted to 2 mL with PBS buffer (pH = 7). Before subjected to fluorescence spectral measurements (λex = 440 nm), the mixed solution was shaken for 3 min to make sure that they reacted completely.
The O. laustris were collected and isolated from LingDe Lake water, Shanxi University, and cultured for one week in a 25 °C incubator.
The C. elegans (N2) were obtained from the College of Life Science at Shanxi University, and cultivated as described in [39].

The E. coli were obtained from the Scientific Instrument Center, Shanxi University, and grown in Lysogeny broth medium (Tryptone 10 g/L, Yeast extract 5 g/L, NaCl 10 g/L) at 37 °C. When the growth reached OD600 = 0.6, the E. coli were inoculated into the tube. The tube was then incubated at 37 °C and grown to OD600 values between 0.50 and 0.55. Finally, the cells were imaged under a ZEISS LSM 880 confocal laser scanning microscope.

The capability of this probe for sensing 4-NP in real water samples was investigated using the standard addition method. First, these samples were filtered by filter papers to remove large particles. Then a series of known standard amounts of 4-NP were dissolved in the tap water samples, and the subsequent fluorescence detection measurements were carried out as described above (Section 2.2).

Fig. 1a is a TEM image of the obtained N-CDs. The N-CDs are microspherical, highly monodispersed particles. The apparent lattice fringes in the HRTEM graph (Fig. 1a, inset) is separated by 0.216 nm, ascribed to the (100) lattice spacing of graphene [40]. The N-CDs size distribution (Fig. 1b) is narrow with a mean diameter of 2.75 nm. The Raman spectra of the N-CDs (Fig. 1c) displays two typical peaks at 1356 and 1582 cm−1, which correspond to the disordered D-band (sp3-hybridized) and crystalline G-band (sp2-hybridized), respectively. The intensity ratio of the D-band to the crystalline G-band (I D/I G) is 0.9778, suggesting a graphite-like structure of the N-CDs [41]. The XRD pattern (Fig. S1) presents an amorphous peak at approximately 24.3° [42].

The surface groups of the as-prepared N-CDs were explored using FT-IR (Fig. 1d). The characteristic peak around 3618 cm−1 is associated with –OH stretching vibrations, and the peaks at 3118 cm−1, 3028 cm−1 may belong to NH and ArH stretching vibrations, respectively [43]. The peaks around 1716 cm−1, 1198 cm−1 reveal the presence of CO and CN, respectively [44]. The absorption band at 1626 cm−1 corresponds to the in-plane bending vibration of NH. The peaks at 1544 cm−1 and 1472 cm−1 represent the skeleton vibration of benzene ring.

The surface element states of the as-prepared N-CDs were investigated by XPS. The three distinct peaks centered at 285.1, 399.1 and 531.1 eV (Fig. 2a) correspond to C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively. This result reveals the relative proportions of the main constituents of the obtained N-CDs: C (68.49%), N (24.67%) and O (6.84%). The C1s spectrum (Fig. 2b) presents three peaks at 284.5, 285.3, 287.5 eV, which can be linked to CC, CN, and CO, respectively [45,46]. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 2c) exhibits three characteristic peaks at around 398.3, 399.7 and 400.1 eV, confirming the presence of C–N–C, CN and NH, respectively [47]. In the O1s spectrum (Fig. 2d), the 531.6 eV and 533.6 eV peaks are assigned to CO and C–OH, respectively [48]. The surface-status information is agreed well with the FT-IR characterization. Simultaneously, the existence of these functional groups endows its favorable solubility.

The corresponding optical performances were also investigated. The absorption band in the UV–vis spectrum of as-prepared N-CDs clearly appears at 276 nm (Fig. 3a), ascribed to π–π* transitions of the CC bond [49]. The photograph (Fig. 3a, inset) shows the N-CDs aqueous solution under visible light (left) and UV illumination at 365 nm (right). Fig. 3b shows the fluorescence emission spectra of the N-CDs at different excitation wavelengths. In the range of wavelengths (400–510 nm) enclosing the maximum excitation wavelength (440 nm), the strongest emission peak appears at 555 nm (Fig. 3a). The excitation-independent property arises from the surface defects and homogeneous microstructure of the N-CDs [50]. Moreover, quantum yield (QY) up to 12.6% at 440 nm excitation, referenced to Rhodamine 6 G (QY = 94% in ethanol).

In this selective study, twenty-six kinds of competitive species (Co2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Cd2+, Cr3+, Fe2+, Pb2+, Zn2+ picric acid, 2,4-dinitrophenol, ammonium nitrate, o-nitrotoluene, o-nitrobenzoic acid, nitrobenzene, 2,4-dichlorophenol, m-nitrobenzoic acid, phenol, m-hydroxybenzoic acid, benzoic acid, benzyl alcohol, salicylic acid, ethyl alcohol, 30 μM) along with 10 μM of 4-NP were chosen to determine their effects on the fluorescence intensity of the system. As depicted in Fig. 4 4-NP leads to the strongest fluorescence quenching, and the influence of other interfering substances is negligible except for picric acid (PA). Nevertheless, we can distinguish between 4-NP and PA based on their different boiling points, which limits PA interfering effects [51]. Above results indicate that the N-CDs possess a distinguished selectivity towards 4-NP.

Fig. 5a presents that the N-CDs fluorescence intensity around 555 nm is stably quenched as the 4-NP concentration increased. In Fig. 5b, the F 0/F is highly correlated with 4-NP concentration corresponding to the regression equation: F 0/F = 0.0309C(4-NP) + 0.9496 (R 2 = 0.995, F 0 and F denote the fluorescence intensities of N-CDs without and with 4-NP, respectively), along with the 4-NP concentration of 0.1–39 μM. The detection limit (LOD) is 0.05 μM (Based on the equation: LOD = 3δ/s, δ and s refer to the standard deviation and the slope of the linear fit, respectively), much lower than the maximum permitted content of 4-NP in drinking water specified by the US Environmental Protection Agency (60 ng/mL, or 0.43 μM). The LOD of various 4-NP-detection methods are compared in Table 1 [52–56]. Clearly, the LOD of this probe is significantly lower than in similar reported works. Thus, the presented N-CDs are a potential probe in practical applications.

To deduce the 4-NP-sensing mechanism of the N-CDs, the quenching efficiency could be expressed by the Stern–Volmer equation:
F 0
/ F =
K sv
Q
+ 1 =
K q
τ 0
Q
+ 1
where F
0 and F are the fluorescence intensities without and with quencher, respectively. K sv is the Stern–Volmer quenching constant and [Q] is the quencher concentration. And K sv = K q τ
0, where K q is the biomolecular quenching constant, and τ
0 is the fluorescence lifetime without quencher. For N-CDs-4-NP system, K sv = 0.0309 μM−1 and τ
0 = 1.191 ns, the K q is calculated to be 2.59 × 1013 M−1 s−1, with higher than the maximum diffusion-controlled quenching rate (2.0 × 1010 M−1 s−1) [57,58]. Meanwhile, the average lifetime of the N-CDs is not distinctly changed before and after adding 4-NP (Fig. S3a), revealing that 4-NP-provoked fluorescence quenching may belong to static quenching.

The relevant UV–vis absorption peaks were measured to prove the above-mentioned deduction. The blue-shifted UV–vis absorption peak of N-CDs, depicted in Fig. S3b, indicates that 4-NP changes the distribution of electrons via affecting N-CDs surface groups, followed by forming a stable non-fluorescent N-CDs–4-NP complex, causing a static fluorescence quenching [59,60].

Tap water samples with known added concentrations of 4-NP (2.0, 4.0, 6.0 and 8.0 μM) were evaluated feasibility and repeatability by utilizing proposed strategy to test the recovery of 4-NP. As revealed in Table 2 , the amount of the 4-NP spiked is nearly in consisence with the content of 4-NP measured in actual sample. Moreover, the recoveries of 98.75–101.50 and the relative standard deviations (RSD) of 1.56–2.50 (n = 5), exemplify that our presented sensor is promising for sensing 4-NP in real water.

Biological toxicity is a significant factor in future applications of N-CDs, especially in biological imaging. Therefore, the in vivo biological toxicity of the N-CDs were evaluated on three model organisms: O. laustris, C. elegans (N2) and E.coli. As shown in Fig. 6 , the viability of O. laustris still keeps over 83% with incubating under a N-CDs concentration as high as 40 mg/mL. Meanwhile, the C. elegans (N2) viability decreases slightly (by 17%) after culturing with 0–80 mg/mL of N-CDs (Fig. S4A), and the E. coli viabilities remains above 85% over the N-CDs concentration range 0–20 mg/mL (Fig. S5A). The above results indicate that N-CDs are less toxic to O. laustris, C. elegans (N2) and E.coli under the experimental conditions at concentrations of 15.12, 2.52 and 22.30 mg/mL, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of N-CDs to biological imaging
ce:section.ce:para: 4-NP is a deleterious nitrophenol compound that is widely used in industries such as drug manufacturing, organic synthesis, and leather preservatives. It is distributed into the environment via various industrial effluents. Its high toxicity and stability continuously affect many organisms, especially aquatic and soil organisms. Therefore, biological imaging of 4-NP is indispensable.

4-NP-dependent fluorescence imaging of the N-CDs was tested on two commonly studied aquatic organisms (O. laustris and E. coli) and one soil organism (C. elegans (N2)), under the confocal laser scanning microscope. The results of these contrasting groups (Fig. 7A, Fig. S4B, Fig. S5B) reveal that N-CDs are a promising fluorescent probe for biological imaging. The real-time dynamic fluorescence quenching process is exhibited in Fig. 7B and Fig. S6. Bright yellow-emitting N-CDs are well dispersed within the living organisms. With the addition of 4-NP, the fluorescence brightness progressively weakened over time, manifesting that our probe can monitor 4-NP within living organisms, thereby extending the applicability of CDs to environmental analysis.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the support for this work from Shanxi Scholarship Council of China (2015-001) and Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province (201801D121074). We also acknowledge the teacher Yan Han and Qi Liu of the School of Life Sciences, Shanxi University for help.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2019.05.022.
